questions.question_text: After a $10\%$ increase, a book costs $\$55$. Find the original price.

options.why: Took $10\%$ off $\$55$ instead of dividing by $110\%$; the original is not $90\%$ of the new price.
Increased $\$55$ by a further $10\%$ instead of working backwards.
Found $10\%$ of $\$55$, the change, rather than the original amount.

questions.question_text: After a $25\%$ decrease, a shirt costs $\$60$. Find the original price.

options.why: Added $25\%$ of $\$60$ instead of dividing by $75\%$; the original is not $125\%$ of the new price.
Took a further $25\%$ off $\$60$ instead of working backwards.
Added the $\$25$ change guess to the sale price rather than using the unitary method.

questions.question_text: After a $20\%$ decrease, a tank has lost $60$ litres of water. How much water did it originally contain?

options.why: Treated $60$ as the $80\%$ remaining instead of as the $20\%$ lost.
Treated $60$ as a $20\%$ decrease of the answer instead of as the change itself.
Found $20\%$ of $60$ instead of scaling $60$ up from $20\%$ to $100\%$.

questions.solution_text:
The $60$ litres lost is the $20\%$ decrease.
$20\% = 60$
$1\% = 60 \div 20 = 3$
$100\% = 3 \times 100$
$= 300$ litres

questions.question_text: After a $12\%$ pay rise, a worker earns $\$168$ a day. A student finds the original pay by taking $12\%$ off $\$168$, getting $\$147.84$. Is this correct?

options.text: No; the original was $\$150$, because $\$168$ is $112\%$ of the original, not $100\%$.
Yes; taking $12\%$ off reverses a $12\%$ increase exactly.
options.why: A $12\%$ increase and a $12\%$ decrease act on different amounts, so they do not cancel.

options.text: No; the original was $\$188.16$, found by adding $12\%$ to $\$168$.
options.why: Adds another increase instead of working back to $100\%$.

options.text: Yes; $\$147.84$ is $88\%$ of $\$168$, which is the original.
options.why: The original is $100\%$ of itself, not $88\%$ of the increased value.

questions.question_text: After a $12\%$ decrease, a town's population is $2200$. Find the original population.

options.why: Added $12\%$ of $2200$ instead of dividing by $88\%$.
Took a further $12\%$ off $2200$ instead of working backwards.
Found $12\%$ of $2200$, the change, rather than the original amount.
